title: Proverbs 21
subtitle: The LORD Weighs the King's Heart; Justice over Sacrifice; Victory Belongs to the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — The LORD Weighs the King's Heart; Justice Over Sacrifice

heb.paragraph: The king's heart is like palgê mayim in the hand of the LORD — he directs it wherever he pleases (v. 1). Palgê are irrigation channels: artificial watercourses that a farmer redirects with a simple turn of the hand. The image is breathtaking in its claim: the most powerful human being is as easily guided by God as water in a ditch.

To do righteousness and mišpāṭ is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice (v. 3). This prophetic principle (cf. 1 Sam 15:22, Mic 6:6–8) appears within the wisdom tradition: ritual observance without ethical conduct is rejected. Mišpāṭ encompasses legal justice, social equity, and right ordering of community life.

refs.note: What to me is the multitude of your sacrifices? — the prophetic parallel to v.3. Justice is what the LORD requires; ritual cannot substitute for it.
The LORD establishes steps — the same sovereignty theme as v.1's king-as-channel.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The watercourse precision — the king who thinks he acts from sovereign will is being channelled by a higher sovereignty. Alter notes the image is agricultural, not militaristic.

Better on a corner of the roof than in a house shared with a quarrelsome wife — the extremity of the contrast is the rhetorical point. Alter notes Proverbs uses this roof-corner image twice (cf. 25:24), emphasising through repetition that relational peace is worth more than comfortable domestic arrangements. Physical discomfort is preferable to psychological corrosion.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The king's heart in the LORD's hand as a watercourse — divine sovereignty over political power is comprehensive. Even the most powerful human will is directional, like an irrigation channel turned wherever its controller chooses.
To do right and just is more acceptable than sacrifice — Proverbs rarely engages the cultic system; when it does, it asserts the priority of ethics over ritual.

Calvin: The king's heart is in the LORD's hand — comfort and warning simultaneously. No earthly power operates outside divine governance; those in positions of authority are responsible to the God who directs them, whether they acknowledge him or not. This prevents both despair at unjust rulers and uncritical deference to any human authority.
Calvin: 'To do what is right is more acceptable than sacrifice — ritual without ethics is an offence, not an offering. The whole prophetic tradition agrees.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note traces the divine-governance-of-kings theme through Isa 10:5-15 (God using Assyria as an instrument), Dan 2:21 (God sets up and deposes kings), and Prov 16:9 (humans plan, God directs). The consistent OT theology: political power is real, human-exercised, and enclosed within comprehensive divine governance.

The NET note connects to 1 Sam 15:22 ('to obey is better than sacrifice'), Ps 40:6-8, and the prophetic critique throughout Amos, Hosea, Isaiah, and Micah. The consistent OT witness: ethics is the substance that cultic practice expresses. Without the substance, the practice is not merely insufficient but offensive.

hist.context: The first half of ch.21 opens with the divine weighing of the king's heart (v.1) — establishing God's sovereignty over all earthly authority. V.3 is the chapter's moral centre: doing what is right and just is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice. The contrast between ritual compliance and genuine justice is the Proverbs echo of the great prophets (Isa 1:11–17; Amos 5:21–24).

sections.header: Verses 16–31 — Diligence; Pride Leads to Conflict; Wisdom Overcomes Strength

heb.paragraph: The plans of the ḥārûṣ (diligent person) lead surely to abundance (v. 5). The root means to be sharp, incisive, decisive — not merely busy but purposefully directed. The contrast with the hasty person (ʾāṣ) who rushes and ends in poverty shows that wisdom values strategic planning over frantic activity.

The wise scale the city of the mighty and bring down the ḥômâ (fortified wall) in which they trust (v. 22). Physical strength is no match for wisdom. The military metaphor inverts expectations: the wise person conquers not through superior force but through superior understanding. Strategy defeats strength.

refs.note: A horse is a vain hope for deliverance — the exact parallel to v.31. The Psalmist and the sage both locate victory with the LORD, not with military preparation.
Pride goes before destruction — the same pride-conflict connection as v.24.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The love-of-pleasure cluster grounds financial ruin in character. Alter notes the accumulation of proverbs on this theme builds a portrait of the person whose desires drive their destruction.
The horse-and-victory proverb — even in warfare, where human preparation matters most, the outcome belongs to the LORD. The sovereignty principle holds at the extreme.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The love-of-pleasure-to-poverty chain — Waltke notes financial ruin in Proverbs traces to character before circumstance. The love of immediate pleasure is the root; poverty is the fruit.
The horse prepared for battle, but victory belongs to the LORD — the sovereignty principle applied to the domain where human preparation and power are most concentrated.
Calvin: 'The righteous gives and does not hold back — this is the natural overflow of the character formed by wisdom. Generosity is not calculated; it is what the wise person is.'
Calvin: 'Safety is of the LORD — use all lawful means (prepare the horse) and commit the outcome entirely to God. To use no means is presumption; to trust the means is idolatry.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the love-of-pleasure proverb connects immediate sensory desire with the poverty it produces. The note traces the pleasure-diligence antithesis through the proverbs collection.
NET: even in the extreme case of warfare, the outcome belongs to the LORD — the human preparation is not invalidated but enclosed within divine determination.

hist.context: The second half addresses diligence versus laziness (vv.25–26), pride as the source of conflict (v.24), and the chapter's climactic verse: no wisdom, insight, or plan can succeed against the LORD (v.30). The divine sovereignty that opened the chapter (v.1) closes it (vv.30–31): human preparation matters, but victory belongs to the LORD.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Guided by integrity, protected by wisdom.

ppl.name: The diligent
ppl.text: Patient productive work yields real gain.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"To do what is right and just is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice."NoteThe prophetic principle applied in wisdom's register. Ritual cannot substitute for justice.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs.
src.note: The editorial note is one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, economics, and social teaching.

rows.label: The LORD Weighs the Heart
rows.text: A person may think their own ways are right, but the LORD weighs the heart. The LORD is the evaluator of what was actually intended, not just what was externally done. The divine judgment is interior, not merely behavioral.

rows.label: Ethics over Ritual
rows.text: To do what is right and just is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice. The consistent OT witness: the substance that sacrifice expresses is more important than the sacrifice itself. When the two conflict, the ethical substance takes priority.

tx.title: Royal and ethical proverbs — LXX rearrangement
tx.content: The LXX rearranges several proverbs in chapter 21 and adds at least two that have no MT equivalent. The added proverbs tend to emphasize divine justice and retribution. MT's order appears more thematically random, while LXX's arrangement suggests deliberate grouping.
tx.note: Whether MT's seemingly random order is original (preserving an older collection) or LXX's thematic grouping is closer to the original arrangement remains one of the open questions in Proverbs textual criticism.

positions.name: Sovereignty is comprehensive
positions.argument: Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus — God directs even pagan rulers. Royal moral responsibility coexists with comprehensive divine governance.

positions.name: God permits but does not directly cause evil
positions.argument: Sovereignty operates through permission and governance of consequences rather than direct causation.
